segments.text: So here's a schematic of how this works.
But basically, if we have some retrieved documents,
we can say generate a summary about Joe Biden.
And when it generates a summary, maybe for the first output,
the language model has high confidence.
And because the language model has high confidence,
we just generate the output.
However, in the next step, it might generate something
like saying Joe Biden attended the University of Pennsylvania.
We're here in the law degree.
But the model might not be very certain about this.
It might have a low probability of certain important entities.
And so based on this, we then form a query,
where what we do is essentially, we blank out
the low probability parts of this, and we do a search.
And so this is also a little bit like the hypothetical document
embedding method, where we basically create a document
that we think will look similar to the document
that we want to find.
We use that to create search results,
and then we generate the output.
And then we continue doing that.
And whenever we have a high confidence output,
like the one here, we don't do any retrieval.
We just generate directly from the parameters of the model.
But whenever we have low confidence outputs,
we do the retrieval and base the output on this.
And so I think this is a nice method
that could potentially be used.
The downside to that is you might sometimes
need to generate twice, because you would generate the output
once and then find the low confidence parts and generate again.
But if you really care about the kind of quality of the output,
this is I think a reasonable thing to do.
OK, so now moving on to the token by token retrieval methods.
The kind of original or one of the methods that popularized
this idea of token by token retrieval is something called K and N L N.
And the way it works is it retrieves similar examples
and then uses the following tokens from these examples.
And this is kind of like a very powerful count-based
bi-gram model in a way.
So if you remember back to what we were talking about,
count-based bi-gram models, what we would do
is we would take the previous token.
And we would calculate the probability
of the next token by summing up together all of the next tokens
and dividing by the total number of times
that previous token occurred.
And so given that background, we can talk
about how the K and N L networks.
So we have the text context, x, and we
want to generate a target output.
Separately from this, we have all of the training contexts.
So this is all of the context that appeared in our training data.
And we encode all of these training contexts
specifically by calculating the representation
of the final layer or near the final layer of the model.
And so we encode that as representations.
Separately from that, we remember the next word that
appeared after this context.
So now we have a data store consisting
of representations in next words.
We then take the representation of the current context.
And we calculate the distance between the current context
and all of the other similar contexts in the database.
We take the nearest K. So we take the top K examples here,
which would be Hawaii, Illinois, and Hawaii.
We then do some sort of normalization
based on the distance.
And this gives us a probability distribution
over all of the next tokens.
Sometimes these tokens are duplicated multiple times.
And so we aggregate all of these counts
to be Hawaii, for example, 0.8 and Illinois 0.2.
And then we interpolate this with the probability
given by the standard language model
using a interpolation coefficient lambda.
And this gives us our final probability.
So the nice thing about this is this allows
us to explicitly ground our outputs in individual examples.
And it's a pretty effective way
to improve the probability of models,
improve translation, and other stuff like this.
The disadvantage of doing this is that it provides,
it kind of adds an extra component of the model.
It adds extra kind of hyperparameters like lambda
and things like this.
So it is a little bit finicky, and it
doesn't work in all situations.
And so another method that we proposed
or by Manapurch, who gave the previous lecture on generation
in this class, is a Lomaformer.
And basically what a Lomaformer does
is it notes that attention itself is an inner product
search, and it does top-k attention.
And the way we do this is we first
process the input with a sliding window
and then perform attention using a vector index.
So if we have a really long input that we want to encode,
what we do is we first encode chunks.
So we encode, for example, AB.
Then we encode CD.
And we encode EF.
We can catenate them together into a big index of one long input.
So in a way that this is similar to what they did in the KMN
and LAM, you know, can catenate all of these embeddings
into a single input.
But the difference is that this is done with the values
that we are attending to is opposed to just the final layer.
And the interesting thing about this
is now we have an index of one long input.
And when we want to do our next version of attention,
we do KMN search from the query.
We take the retrieve hidden states,
and then we just do attention over that.
So the nice thing about this is in the extreme case,
this makes no changes to the model.
What I mean by this is, let's say,
it was small enough that we could encode it in only a single chunk.
And for KMN search, we also did KMN search.
We did exact KMN search over all of the embeddings in the trunk.
In that case, this would just be normal attention.
It's exactly the same as normal attention.
However, there are some approximations that go into here.
Like when we encode chunks, they might not be exactly the same
if we encoded the entire thing together.
And we're also chopping off some of the values
with very low head of inner products.
And so because of this,
there are some approximations being made,
but in the extreme case, if we'd made no approximations,
this would just be exactly the same model
as we were using before.
So I find this pretty attractive,
and empirically it gives very good results
over long distances.
And we can always make our approximations better
and improve this model as well.
So I think this is a attractive method
that you might be interested in taking.
Okay, for the final part of this,
I'd like to talk about long context transformers.
And these are models that are explicitly trained
in a way that allows you to attend to longer contexts
in an efficient manner.
So one way that we can train over longer contexts
is just append all of the contexts together.
And in fact, shortly after transformers came out,
this paper by Voida, it all demonstrated that
doing this can learn, you know,
interesting document level phenomena.
So it can identify when multiple words
refer to the same thing or co-reference
and other things like this.
However, the problem with transformers
is that computation is quadratic
and the sentence length
because you're multiplying all of the query vectors
by all of the key vectors.
And that basically causes a big problem
if your sequence is become very long.
So if we go back to what we did in RNNs
from the very beginning of the class,
in RNNs they don't have this problem
because computation is linear in the length of the sequence.
You just pass along the RNN state
and every single time you do the same computation over it.
So there's no quadratic term in calculating RNNs.
Another thing is that when doing RNNs,
you can actually pass state infinitely
during the forward pass by just calculating the hidden state
and then throwing away the rest of the computation graph
that was used in calculating that hidden state.
And there's no approximation that goes on there.
So unlike in an aluminum former that I was talking about
before, we needed to make approximations
not need to be made in this case.
However, there is a problem with doing back-proP,
because in...
